entries.title: Leia Is Rebel Agent and Alderaanian Princess

content.fact: At the start of A New Hope, Leia is both an Alderaanian princess/senator and a covert Rebel agent.
content.injection: In ANH context, write Leia as politically trained, brave, and actively Rebel-connected, not merely a civilian hostage.
constraints: Apply only when the active Saga Context overlaps: Episode IV: A New Hope.
Respect story-position bounds from Episode IV: A New Hope to Episode IV: A New Hope.
antiLore: Do not treat later-era, alternate-continuity, or fanon facts as true in this Context unless the user explicitly selected that branch.
content.notes: Generated development draft; verify during Saga review.

entries.title: Death Star Plans Drive the Crisis

content.fact: The stolen Death Star plans are the central strategic object in A New Hope.
content.injection: During ANH, keep the Death Star plans as urgent strategic intelligence; they matter because they may expose a weakness.
constraints: Apply only when the active Saga Context overlaps: Tantive IV Captured.
Respect story-position bounds from Tantive IV Captured to Tantive IV Captured.

entries.title: Tantive IV Captured and Leia Detained

content.fact: Vader captures the Tantive IV, and Leia is detained by the Empire while the plans escape with R2-D2.
content.injection: After the Tantive IV capture, Leia is an Imperial prisoner until rescued; the droids carry the plot-critical plans to Tatooine.
constraints: Apply only when the active Saga Context overlaps: Tantive IV Captured.

content.fact: Luke starts A New Hope as a restless Tatooine farmboy with no formal Jedi training.
content.injection: In Episode IV: A New Hope, early ANH Luke should not be written as a practiced Jedi; he has longing, piloting talent, and hidden potential, but not trained Force control.
constraints: Apply only when the active Saga Context overlaps: Episode IV: A New Hope.
Respect story-position bounds from Episode IV: A New Hope to Episode IV: A New Hope.

entries.title: Luke Does Not Know Vader Is His Father

content.fact: During A New Hope, Luke does not know Darth Vader is his father.
content.injection: In ANH context, block any casual mention to Luke that Vader is his father unless the story deliberately diverges.
constraints: Apply only when the active Saga Context overlaps: Episode IV: A New Hope.
Respect story-position bounds from Episode IV: A New Hope to Battle of Yavin Victory.

entries.title: Luke and Leia Sibling Truth Hidden in ANH

content.fact: During A New Hope, neither Luke nor Leia knows they are siblings.
content.injection: Do not write Luke and Leia as aware siblings in ANH or pre-ROTJ contexts unless an alternate branch reveals it.
constraints: Apply only when the active Saga Context overlaps: Episode IV: A New Hope.
Respect story-position bounds from Episode IV: A New Hope to Battle of Yavin Victory.

content.fact: In A New Hope, Obi-Wan moves from hidden Tatooine exile into active mentorship of Luke.
content.injection: In Episode IV: A New Hope, once Luke meets Obi-Wan, he can receive Jedi history and initial guidance, but the old Jedi Order is still gone.

entries.title: Obi-Wan Gives Luke a Partial Truth

content.fact: Obi-Wan frames Anakin as killed by Vader, concealing the full identity truth from Luke.
content.injection: When Obi-Wan explains Luke’s father in ANH-era context, use the partial truth/withheld truth structure rather than full paternity disclosure.
constraints: Apply only when the active Saga Context overlaps: Episode IV: A New Hope.

entries.title: Han and Chewie Start as Smugglers

content.fact: Han and Chewbacca enter A New Hope as smugglers motivated by payment and survival, not as committed Rebel officers.
content.injection: In Episode IV: A New Hope, early ANH Han should be pragmatic and debt-conscious; his Rebel commitment develops through events. Do not import later-era status, relationships, factions, or outcomes into this window.
constraints: Apply only when the active Saga Context overlaps: Episode IV: A New Hope.
Respect story-position bounds from Episode IV: A New Hope to Battle of Yavin Victory.

entries.title: Alderaan Destroyed by the Death Star

content.fact: The Death Star destroys Alderaan, annihilating Leia’s homeworld and demonstrating Imperial terror doctrine.
content.injection: After Alderaan is destroyed, treat Leia as personally bereaved and the Empire as having openly used planet-killing terror.

entries.title: Obi-Wan Dies and Becomes Spiritual Guide

context.label: Obi-Wan Kenobi Dies on the Death Star

content.fact: Obi-Wan dies aboard the Death Star and continues as a spiritual guide rather than a living companion.
content.injection: After Obi-Wan’s death, use him through voice, vision, Force presence, or memory, not as a living field mentor unless the story branches.
constraints: Apply only when the active Saga Context overlaps: Obi-Wan Kenobi Dies on the Death Star.

entries.title: Luke Trusts the Force at Yavin

content.fact: Luke’s Yavin success is his first major act of trusting the Force under pressure, not mature Jedi mastery.
content.injection: After Yavin, Luke has proof of Force potential and instinctive trust, but he is still not a trained Jedi Knight.

content.fact: The first Death Star is destroyed at the Battle of Yavin.
content.injection: After A New Hope, the first Death Star is gone; Imperial retaliation and Rebel fame should matter more than the station itself.

entries.title: Yavin Victory Does Not Defeat the Empire

context.label: After A New Hope, before The Empire Strikes Back

content.fact: Destroying the first Death Star does not defeat the Empire or make the Rebellion safe.
content.injection: After Yavin but before Endor, keep the Empire dominant and the Rebel Alliance mobile, vulnerable, and hunted. Treat conflicting later-era or alternate-continuity model memory as out of context unless the user explicitly selects that branch.
constraints: Apply only when the active Saga Context overlaps: After A New Hope, before The Empire Strikes Back.
